---
title: "Exercises Day 3"
author:
- name: Ronald Geskus
  affiliations:
    - OUCRU biostat
- name: Tran Thai Hung
  affiliations:
    - OUCRU biostat
date: 'last-modified'
date-format: '[Last version:] MMMM DD, YYYY'
format: 
  pdf:
    fontsize: 12pt
toc: TRUE
lof: FALSE
lot: FALSE
number-sections: FALSE
thanks: "Please do not circulate."
abstract: ""
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  warning: false
---

## Instructions

Import the data set via the code

```{r echo=T, eval=T}
library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)
titanic <- read_excel("../slides/data/Titanic3.xlsx", na="NA")
```

## Exercise 1

Plot the price that was paid (variable `fare`) as a function of age. Assign/map the appropriate variable to the x-axis and y-axis. Next, define the geometric object. Change the label of fare into "fare paid (GBP)". In R: `ggplot(..., aes(...)) + geom_point(...) + ylab(...)` See what happens if you change the coordinate system to polar via `+coord_polar()`.

```{r warning=TRUE}
ggplot(titanic, aes(age, fare)) + geom_point() + ylab("fare paid (GBP)")
ggplot(titanic, aes(age, fare)) + geom_point() + 
  ylab("fare paid (GBP)") + coord_polar()
```

## Exercise 2
Return to the cartesian coordinate system. Add a second layer that fits the linear regression line with 95% confidence intervals through the scatterplot (this is seen as smoothing, choose as method `lm`). Change the overall theme to `bw` and `minimal`. In R: add `... + geom_smooth(method="lm") + theme_bw()`. Save the graph as a png file.

```{r}
ggplot(titanic, aes(age, fare)) + geom_point() + 
  ylab("fare paid (GBP)") + geom_smooth(method="lm") + theme_minimal()
ggplot(titanic, aes(age, fare)) + geom_point() + 
  ylab("fare paid (GBP)") + geom_smooth(method="lm") + theme_bw()
ggsave("SavedPlot.png")
```

## Exercise 3
The distribution of fare is very skewed. Therefore, it may be better to plot fare on a logarithmic scale. Repeat the exercise but now use a log scale along the fare axis. If you compare the fitted linear trends, you will notice that they do not coincide. This is for two reasons. First, there were persons that didn't pay any money. They are removed if the logarithm is taken (you cannot take the log of zero). Second, the regression line is computed after the scale transformation, hence based on very different values. In R replace `ylab(...)` by `scale_y_log10(...)`

```{r warning=TRUE}
ggplot(titanic, aes(age, fare)) + geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method="lm") + theme_bw() + 
  scale_y_log10("fare paid (GBP)")
```

## Exercise 4
We continue with the graph from the last exercise that uses the log scale for fare. Color the individual data by passenger class. Split the calculation and plot of the linear regression line by these three groups. In R: add `color=pclass` to the aesthetic attributes.

```{r}
ggplot(titanic, aes(age, fare, color=pclass)) + geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method="lm") + theme_bw() + 
  scale_y_log10("fare paid (GBP)")
```

## Exercise 5
Split the graph into two separate ones by sex, next to each other. Add a rug plot to both sides. In R add `... + facet_wrap(~sex) + geom_rug()`

```{r}
ggplot(titanic, aes(age, fare, color=pclass)) + geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method="lm") + theme_bw() +
  scale_y_log10("fare paid (GBP)") + facet_wrap(~sex) + geom_rug()
```

## Exercise 6
Make a boxplot of age (along y-axis) for each of the passenger classes (along x-axis). Change the label of the pclass variable into "passenger class". In R: `ggplot(..., aes(...)) + geom_boxplot(...) + xlab(...)` See what happens if you flip the coordinates via `coord_flip()`.

```{r}
ggplot(titanic, aes(pclass, age)) + geom_boxplot() +
  xlab("passenger class")
ggplot(titanic, aes(pclass, age)) + geom_boxplot() +
  xlab("passenger class") + coord_flip()
```

## Exercise 7
Add the individual data points to the boxplot. Use the jitter instead of the point geometric object to be able to discriminate them, give the points a red color and make them transparent by setting the transparency parameter (alpha) to 0.3. In R: add `... + geom_jitter(color="red", alpha=0.2)`. Replace the boxplot by a violin plot.

```{r}
ggplot(titanic, aes(pclass, age)) + geom_boxplot() +
  xlab("passenger class") + geom_jitter(color="red",alpha=0.2) + 
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
# If you don't jitter the points, you get the following:
ggplot(titanic, aes(pclass, age)) + geom_boxplot() + 
  xlab("passenger class") + geom_point(color="red",alpha=0.2) + 
  theme_minimal()
```


```{r}
# Violin plot instead of boxplot
ggplot(titanic, aes(pclass, age)) + geom_violin() + 
  xlab("passenger class") + geom_jitter() + theme_minimal()
```

## Exercise 8
We make a new graph in which we perform an exploratory analysis of the relation between survival and age, sex and passenger class. Define the assignment/mapping to the x-axis (`age`) and y-axis (`survived`). Make a scatterplot of survival status as a function of age.

In order to better see the individual outcomes, use jitter with a height parameter of 0.05 and a width parameter of 0; set transparency alpha to 0.5. Add a smooth layer using the **loess** method with 95% confidence intervals.

```{r}
ggplot(titanic, aes(age,survived)) + 
  geom_point() + geom_smooth(method="loess")
```

## Exercise 9
Create separate loess curves for males and females. Put the legend above the graph.

```{r}
ggplot(titanic, aes(age,survived,colour=sex)) + 
  geom_jitter(height=0.05,alpha=0.5) + geom_smooth(method="loess") + 
  theme(legend.position='top')
```


```{r}
# Extra: we change the label and the colour palette
ggplot(titanic, aes(age,survived,colour=sex)) + 
  geom_jitter(height=0.05,alpha=0.5) + geom_smooth(method="loess") +
  scale_colour_brewer(name="gender",label=c("F","M"),palette='Set1') + 
  theme(legend.position='top')
```

## Exercise 10
Split the graph into three panels based on passenger class. Truncate the range of the y-axis. Do this by setting the y-axis limits to c(-0.1,1.1) and see what happens.

```{r}
ggplot(titanic, aes(age,survived,colour=sex)) + 
  geom_jitter(height=0.05,alpha=0.3) + geom_smooth(method="loess") +
  facet_wrap(~pclass) + ylim(c(-0.1,1.1)) +  theme(legend.position='top')
```


```{r}
# Truncating the output is prevented by setting the lim its within coord_cartesian
ggplot(titanic, aes(age,survived,colour=sex)) + 
  geom_jitter(height=0.05,alpha=0.3) + geom_smooth(method="loess") +
  facet_wrap(~pclass) + coord_cartesian(ylim=c(-0.1,1.1))
```
